results.snippet: Authoritative empirical guidance from NN/G: cards work best as summaries of heterogeneous content (exactly the mod-card use-case), but are less scannable than lists because element positioning is less predictable for the eye. Multicard displays still obey page-hierarchy laws — content at top-left is perceived as most important — which directly informs where to put the mod name and category badge. NN/G also notes that even though cards de-emphasize ranking, top-of-card placement signals primacy.
results.title: Cards: UI-Component Definition — Nielsen Norman Group

results.snippet: NN/G head-to-head comparison: list view is more scannable and space-efficient; card view is better for visual grouping. For 291 mod cards, this is the core trade-off. Practical implication: offering a density toggle (card ↔ list) recovers scannability when users shift from browsing to searching. Also establishes that cards take more vertical space, so field count inside each card must be ruthlessly pruned.
results.title: Card View vs. List View — NN/G Video

results.snippet: Practitioner synthesis on when to use cards vs. lists vs. tables for large catalogs. Covers information density thresholds, sorting/filtering considerations, and when metadata (like download count) is better surfaced in a list column than squeezed into a card. Relevant because 291 items sits right at the density inflection point where a list-toggle matters most.
results.title: Table vs List View vs Card Grid: Which Data Display Pattern Should You Use? — UX Patterns for Developers

results.snippet: Practitioner article covering field weight and ordering: title should be noticeably larger than description; CTA must be visually distinct from everything else; secondary metadata (like download count and category badge) should use reduced visual weight so they don't compete with the primary action. Directly applicable to ordering the five fields — name > category badge > download count > description excerpt > button.
results.title: 8 Best Practices for UI Card Design — UX Collective

results.snippet: Three-layer framework (UX intent → visual hierarchy → component structure) with specific guidance on badge anchoring: if a card has a preview image, anchor the badge to the lower-right of the image overlay; without an image, place it in the card header left-aligned with the title. Download count and CTA go in the footer zone. Maps cleanly onto the five mod-card fields.
results.title: Successful Card Design in 3 Steps: UX, UI, and Framework — UX Collective

results.snippet: Research-backed piece on CTA placement: bottom-of-card positioning reduces decision latency because it matches thumb reach zones and the natural read sequence (scan name → read description → act). For a dense 291-card grid, bottom-anchored CTAs also create visual rhythm across the grid, making the action zone predictable without re-reading each card. Directly relevant to where the Download button should live.
results.title: Optimal Placement for Mobile Call to Action Buttons — UX Movement
